projects.name: Untitled Blacksmith Game
projects.overview: This blacksmith game is a simple co-op friendslop game where you and your friends start off in a blacksmith shop and you have to complete orders in order to gain money and keep the shop running. As you progress through the game you will be able to upgrade your shop, unlock new ores, and new recipes that you can sell to your customers. This draws inspiration from the wave of friendslop co-op games like Peak and Lethal Company.
projects.goals: My goal for this game is to develop my skills in game developement and design. I want to create a fun and engaging game that my friends and I can play together.
projects.responsibilities:
Goal

 My goal for this game is to develop my skills in game development and design. I want to create a fun and engaging game that my friends and I can play together. Where I can primarily focus on the development side, making a modular system where if I want to add a product, like a halberd, all I would need to do is add the recipe objects for the final product, and any corresponding recipes for the pieces of the halberd. I initially started this projec tin Unity. However, after working in Unity for most of the project, I wanted to widen my skills and decided to shift into making the game in Unreal. I have now spent the last few weeks working on it. I was able to catch up on 2 months of work fairly quickly, as the blueprint system felt fairly intuitive.

Base Interaction Parent

 The initial step for this game was to create the basic interaction of picking up items, as that will be the main action the player will be doing throughout the game. This interaction function will also be used to interact with levers, buttons, and the shop to buy new recipes and unlock new ores. The player controller contains the Interact function, which sends a blueprint interface message to any blueprint that has the Interact Message Event node. Compared to Unity, it is a lot cleaner setup as there is not an excess of scripts on every object.

Work Stations

  The anvil, furnace, chopping block, assembly station, and shop all have their own data tables, with them all containing structs. Each one is geared towards what the player can put on/in them, and then looks through the data table to show the player the possible outputs. One version is the assembly station, which will look through what the two input pieces will generate. The anvil will take the ingot that the player drops on it and look through the data table of possible recipes, where if the player gives an iron ingot, the only outputs shown will be things such as an Iron Sword Blade and an Iron Dagger Blade. I also added a small UI widget to show off the possible outcomes to the player. The anvil also has an interactive function on it, where the player can index through the list of recipes.

Data Tables/Structs vs. Lists/Serializable Objects

  In Unreal, I utilize Data Tables with Structs to allow myself to add new items on the fly without creating a hassle for issues with blueprints causing issues. Compared to Unity’s List with Serializable Objects, I did find it slightly easier to use the structs in comparison to serializable objects.
projects.tools: C#, Blender, Shader Graph, Visual Effects Graph

projects.name: Ninjas On Trampolines
projects.overview:
Ninjas on Trampolines is a physics-based platform fighter where players play as Ninjas that can paint their own colors on the wall. The objective of the game is to have the most of your own color on the back wall by the end of a match, which can be done by smacking other players or using a spray can to graffiti the background.
 The game features three different game modes: Free-for-All, Spray King, and Paintball. Each gamemode takes a twist on the simple, yet extremely captivating game loop of Free-for-All by introducing new objectives for players. Paintball has players chase a soccer ball-like object, while Spray King has players chase another player holding an item that gives them an advantage.
projects.responsibilities:
During the first few months of development, my main priority was engineering the core gameplay systems, including the foundational movement controller and animation state machines. My main focus, however, was designing and programming the dynamic paint system, which servers as the core feedback mechanic for combat.

Learning Paint Shader

 The core design philosophy behind the paint system was to give players immediate, lasting feedback on where skimishes happened across the map. Initially, I developed a system that masked textures to spawn static paint circles where an opponent was killed. However, to make combat feel more impactful, I redesigned the system to be velocity-based after player feedback.

 On the backend, I wrote scripts to capture the player's velocity vector at the exact moment of a kill. This data is then passed directly into the material properties. Initially painting multiple splatters onto the background caused a massive spike in GPU draw calls, which tanked the performance of the game. To help in optimizing the game I refactored the code to dynamically scale and orient a single texture based on that incoming speed and direction data. In which it improved the performance while makign the splatter shape unique to every kill. I also handled the programming for basic scene transitions, audio triggers, and music hooks to tie the combat loop together.

 A major design challenge we faced was ensuring visual readability for all players, as the gameplay relies heavily on identifying eight distinct paint colors. To address this, I developed a custom post-processing system featuring filters for the three most common types of color blindness: Protanopia, Tritanopia, and Deuteranopia.

 From a logic standpoint, I realized that simply altering colors wasn't enough, as some hues still blended together. To solve this, I coded a layered solution that applies a mathematical contrast filter and assigns distinct texture patterns to specific players' paint. Recently I updated the equation driving the color filter effect and exposed a color shift variable to the UI. This allows the players to dynamically adjust the backend filter values to their specific need, ensuring the game state is readable for everyone.
projects.tools: C#, HLSL, Shader Graph, Visual Effects Graph

imageGallery.subtitle: Color filter for the 8 player colors

projects.name: PROJECT: Gladiator
projects.overview:
Project: Gladiator is a two-player co-op game that pits players against each other in intense 2v2 combat. Teams must rely on communication and synergy to emerge victorious and survive the brutal arena battles. Matches bring four duos into a Colosseum-inspired setting, capturing the grandeur and intensity of ancient gladiatorial contests.
 Drawing inspiration from the movie Gladiator, the game introduces a unique mechanic influenced by Chained Together and Bred & Fred, combined with the deliberate combat style of Souls-like games such as Elden Ring and Dark Souls.
 Players are tethered to their partners, restricting movement to within a fixed range. This constraint applies to all actions, including backstepping and rolling, emphasizing coordination between teammates. Combat unfolds from a third-person perspective, featuring light and heavy attacks, as well as intricate combos, all designed to deliver a visceral and rewarding experience.
 Project: Gladiator challenges players to master the art of cooperation while embracing the high-stakes tension of the arena.
projects.responsibilities:
I am the sole developer of Project: Gladiator. The combat system is heavily inspired by Souls-like games because I have always admired the deliberate, tactical feel of their combat. However, I wanted to create something that emphasizes cooperation, which is why I introduced the chain mechanic tethering players together. This feature forces players to rely on each other, creating a uniquely challenging and rewarding co-op experience.
 I envisioned the gameplay to reflect the tension and stakes of gladiatorial combat, much like Maximus's first fight in Gladiator. That scene left a lasting impression on me, and I wanted to capture its raw energy and life-or-death intensity within the game. Each match begins with players starting in different parts of the arena, with only the last surviving team emerging victorious.
 Co-op games like It Takes Two, Chained Together, and Bread and Fred inspired me because of how engaging they are for players and spectators alike. I noticed how these games often generate moments of stress, frustration, and teamwork that are both entertaining to play and fun to watch. Seeing content creators share their experiences with these games influenced my decision to incorporate similar dynamics into Project: Gladiator.
 This game is my way of combining everything I love about challenging combat, cooperative gameplay, and the emotional highs and lows that make these experiences memorable.
projects.tools: C#, Shader Graph, Visual Effects Graph

projects.name: Doodle Day
projects.overview: Doodle Day is a simulation made in 24 hours for the Wolfjam Hackathon 2023. THe primary goal of the experience is to use a rubber mallet to defeat aliens that appear on your screen. However, there is a news tab that reveals a darker side of what you are doing.  The game ends as you destory Earth.
projects.tools: C#, Shader Graph, Blender, Visual Effects Graph

projects.name: Olympus Interactive Site
projects.overview: This website is a side-project of mine  to allow my small group of developers to have a homepage for our work. It was designed in a short time and was an oppurtunity for me to polish on by web design skills with Figma.
projects.responsibilities: I was responsible for the design the page. I utilized Figma, to design and framework a basic website to showcase the project that my group has worked on. I mainly did the design work, while the main developer worked on the site.
